title: Barcodes and $C^0$ symplectic topology
speaker: Sobhan Seyfaddini
abstract: Hamiltonian homeomorphisms are those homeomorphisms of a symplectic manifold which can be written as uniform limits of Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms. One difficulty in studying Hamiltonian homeomorphisms (particularly in dimensions greater than two) has been that we possess fewer tools for studying them. For example, (filtered) Floer homology, which has been a very effective tool for studying Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms, is not well-defined for homeomorphisms.

proof_stages.label: Aspherical Hamiltonian setup and spectral norm
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a closed connected aspherical symplectic manifold, defines Hamiltonian homeomorphisms as the C^0-closure of Ham, recalls filtered Hamiltonian Floer homology and spectral numbers, and packages the extremal spectral numbers into the conjugation-invariant spectral norm gamma.

proof_stages.label: C^0-continuity theorem for gamma
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem, joint with Buhovsky and Humiliere, states that gamma is continuous for the C^0 topology on Hamiltonian diffeomorphisms and extends continuously to Hamiltonian homeomorphisms, generalizing earlier cases.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to local-support continuity
proof_stages.summary: To prove the theorem it suffices to prove continuity at the identity, and the known 2011 result gives this when the Hamiltonian diffeomorphism fixes an open set pointwise.

proof_stages.label: C^0 fragmentation by a small local inverse
proof_stages.summary: A fragmentation lemma would produce, for a C^0-small phi, a C^0-small Hamiltonian psi supported in a chosen ball and inverse to phi on another ball, so that the triangle inequality reduces gamma(phi) to the local-support case.

proof_stages.label: Higher-dimensional product trick
proof_stages.summary: Since the required fragmentation is hard in higher symplectic dimensions, the proof applies a weak fragmentation to the special product map Phi=phi x phi^{-1} and uses a Kunneth-type Floer formula gamma(Phi)=2 gamma(phi) to complete the reduction.

proof_stages.label: Spectral differences and barcodes up to shift
proof_stages.summary: Although individual mean-normalized spectral numbers are not C^0-continuous, differences of spectral numbers are bounded by gamma and hence extend, which in barcode language gives filtered Floer homology of Hamiltonian homeomorphisms up to an overall shift.

proof_stages.label: Arnold conjecture reformulated for Hamiltonian homeomorphisms
proof_stages.summary: Using the extended spectral data, the talk replaces the false naive fixed-point count by a statement that if a Hamiltonian homeomorphism has too few spectral invariants then its fixed-point set is infinite, thereby recovering Arnold's bound in the smooth case.

proof_stages.label: Rokhlin-property obstruction
proof_stages.summary: After defining Rokhlin topological groups as those with a dense conjugacy class, the continuity and conjugation invariance of gamma show that Hamiltonian homeomorphism groups are not Rokhlin, since the identity cannot lie in the closure of a nontrivial conjugacy class.

examples.name: Small-support high Hamiltonian counterexample
examples.note: Hamiltonians supported in balls of radius 1/i but with large normalized height converge C^0 to the identity while individual normalized spectral numbers need not, showing why only differences such as gamma can be C^0-continuous.
examples.engagement_reason: He constructs Hamiltonians supported in balls of radius 1/i, checks the C^0 distance goes to zero, normalizes by subtracting the integral, and computes the resulting spectral invariant behavior.

examples.name: Identity map with one spectral value
examples.note: The identity Hamiltonian diffeomorphism illustrates that counting spectral invariants alone cannot imply Arnold's lower bound, since all its spectral values coincide.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses the identity only as a quick instance where all spectral invariants coincide, with no further calculation.
